authoritative_sources.title: Safety and Health Regulations for Construction
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator. Part 1926 is the controlling federal baseline for construction safety duties, inspections, hazard controls, and stop conditions that scheduling cannot override.
used_for: refusal of safety-signoff or execution authority

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Process
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City School Construction Authority
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC SCA publishes detailed public-owner construction workflow guidance covering project schedules, permits, site safety plans, subcontractor approvals, closeout, and required deliverables for active construction projects.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Demand intake, schedule control, permit handling, and closeout planning
used_for: site safety plan and closeout context

authoritative_sources.title: Obtain a Certificate of Occupancy
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is the authoritative local permitting and occupancy regulator. This guidance defines final approval, temporary occupancy, and unresolved-issue boundaries that can materially gate scheduled turnover and closeout timing.

authoritative_sources.title: Residential Building Final Inspection Quick Sheet
authoritative_sources.publisher: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official final-inspection quick sheet provides a concrete public baseline for approved plans on site, associated permit closure, special inspection letters, and test results required before final inspection and completion.

authoritative_sources.title: P120 Project Cost and Schedule Management Requirements for the Public Buildings Service
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA publishes an official federal schedule-governance directive defining baseline, milestone, CPM, update, and re-baseline expectations for public construction scheduling.

authoritative_sources.title: Use our project schedule management for small projects
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official GSA workflow reference defines initial and baseline schedule timing, stakeholder communication cadence, and common schedule-extension drivers for smaller projects and field work.

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Manual Section 3-5 Control of Work
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Caltrans publishes recognized public construction operations guidance covering order of work, progress schedules, contractor assignments, and subcontracting requests, making it authoritative for sequence control and subcontractor-related scheduling boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Sequence control and subcontractor dependency management

workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and scheduling-frame normalization
workflow_stages.description: Classify incoming work demand, normalize site, work-order, permit, and owner-readiness context, and confirm that the request is specific enough for planning review.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess crews, subcontractors, access windows, milestones, permits, inspections, site conditions, equipment, and service-level limits before recommending timing or allocation.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Sequence and booking recommendation support
workflow_stages.description: Generate recommendation-only look-ahead sequencing, booking-readiness, and deferral logic using approved priority rules, baseline schedules, and documented tradeoffs.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Permit, inspection, and site-readiness gating
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether work can be scheduled or committed based on permit approval, approved plans, associated permit closure, safety posture, inspection status, and predecessor evidence.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Turnover, closeout, and approval escalation
workflow_stages.description: Block or defer completion-sensitive schedule commitments when occupancy, closeout, punch-list, or approval dependencies remain unresolved, and route tradeoffs to the correct owner.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: work orders, service requests, backlog queues, and booking requests
baseline schedules, look-ahead plans, milestone trackers, recovery plans, and delay notes
permit records, approved plans, associated permit status, and certificate or turnover dependencies
inspection records, special inspection letters, test reports, and deficiency logs
site logs, safety observations, access approvals, utility readiness records, and weather-impact notes
crew calendars, subcontractor commitments, equipment or lift allocations, and service-level targets
change orders, RFIs, punch lists, closeout packages, and owner-readiness records
systems_of_record: field service, dispatch, or work-order systems
scheduling tools and baseline schedule repositories
tenant approval matrices and policy libraries

allowed: Classify scheduling work as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Assess whether available records support a bounded timing, routing, booking, or allocation recommendation.
Produce evidence-backed schedule recommendations, capacity summaries, constraint logs, and approval-needed packages without claiming final approval authority.
escalate: Required permit, inspection, site, schedule, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
A safety deficiency, failed inspection, permit condition, or occupancy dependency changes whether work may proceed.
The request requires final staffing, budget, permit, code, trade, superintendent, or operational approval authority.
Public baseline guidance and tenant or jurisdiction policy materially conflict on scheduling precedence, turnover timing, or closeout gating.
refuse: Commit final schedule approval, overtime approval, staffing approval, or service-level degradation approval.
Approve permits, inspections, occupancy, code compliance, or licensed trade work without explicit authority.
Schedule work as ready when permit, inspection, safety, or owner-readiness blockers remain unresolved.

domain_failure_modes: booking work without permit approval, approved plans, or predecessor completion evidence
committing labor or subcontractor windows without verified capacity, site access, or owner-readiness state
allowing schedule pressure to override a failed inspection, safety stop, or unresolved permit condition
treating turnover or closeout dates as firm while punch-list, occupancy, or closeout evidence remains incomplete
inventing tie-break rules when competing priorities lack an approved decision rule
collapsing construction scheduling into generic calendar management without documenting subcontractor, site, and inspection dependencies
acting as code interpreter, licensed trade authority, or final approver instead of escalating to the correct owner

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Site or work-order handling with prerequisites and evidence capture
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit closure, temporary occupancy, and final-inspection sequencing rules vary materially across local authorities and project types.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline establishes that permit, inspection, and occupancy evidence can gate schedule commitments, but runtime must still retrieve local AHJ rules, owner contract requirements, and tenant escalation policy before asserting project-specific turnover timing.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant labor agreements, shift rules, and self-perform versus subcontractor staffing controls can materially change feasible schedule options.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This is resolved operationally by requiring tenant-specific staffing rules, service levels, and approval matrices as mandatory runtime inputs before deterministic allocation recommendations.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, authority boundaries, and failure handling instead of generic calendar or route optimization only.

The lane remains recommendation-only even when it produces detailed sequence or capacity guidance; superintendent, project-controls, permitting, inspection, safety, and licensed-trade owners retain approval authority outside the lane.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because all known variation is explicitly handled through runtime retrieval and escalation requirements.
